sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Vaginal cancer typically presents in individuals assigned female at birth (AFAB) over 70 years of age, with the majority of invasive cases being squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), accounting for 80%–90% of cases, most commonly arising in the upper portion of the posterior vaginal wall [1,2]. The most significant signs are vaginal bleeding (often postcoital or irregular), discharge, urine retention, and rectal symptoms such as constipation or blood in the stool. However, up to 20% of patients may be asymptomatic, with disease discovered incidentally during pelvic examination, Pap cytology, colposcopy, or vulvar screening [3]. A cofactor history includes immunosuppression, prior hysterectomy, and cigarette smoking. Vaginal cancer is frequently synchronous or metachronous with cervical cancer, and only a minority are primary vaginal cancers; the remainder are metastatic from other sites [3].

symptoms.name: Vaginal bleeding
symptoms.detail: Often postcoital, irregular, or postmenopausal bleeding; may be the initial presenting symptom.

symptoms.name: Vaginal discharge
symptoms.detail: May be bloody, watery, or purulent, and can be malodorous.

symptoms.name: Dyspareunia
symptoms.detail: Pain during intercourse, often due to vaginal mass or ulceration.

symptoms.name: Pelvic pain
symptoms.detail: May be due to local tumor extension, pelvic sidewall involvement, or hydronephrosis.

symptoms.name: Urinary symptoms
symptoms.detail: Dysuria, frequency, urgency, or retention due to bladder or urethral involvement.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; may indicate advanced tumor [3].

symptoms.name: Rectal symptoms
symptoms.detail: Constipation, tenesmus, or rectal bleeding from tumor invasion of the rectum or rectovaginal septum.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; seen in advanced disease [3].

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Unexplained vaginal bleeding, especially in postmenopausal individuals, requires urgent evaluation.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent or bloody discharge warrants investigation.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: New-onset dyspareunia in older adults should raise suspicion.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Persistent or worsening pelvic pain indicates possible advanced disease.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Urinary retention or severe dysuria requires urgent assessment.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Rectal bleeding or obstruction is an alarm feature.
